title: Data Cleaning
text: ’ll simulate study dataset kinds problems actually show : duplicate rows faulty join, columns nearly NA, outliers data-entry errors, measure collected multiple visits, continuous variable needs binning downstream analysis.
code: set.seed(7)  n <- 100 raw_data <- tibble::tibble(   subject_id     = c(sprintf("S%03d", 1:n), "S042", "S073"),  # two duplicates   age            = c(round(rnorm(n, 65, 8), 1), 67, 71),   bmi            = c(round(rnorm(n, 26, 4), 1), 27, 24),   weight_kg      = c(round(rnorm(n, 75, 12), 1), 250, 78),    # one outlier   almost_empty   = c(rep(NA_real_, n - 1), 1.5, NA, NA),   fully_empty    = rep(NA_real_, n + 2),   dsst_v1        = round(rnorm(n + 2, 50, 10)),   dsst_v2        = ifelse(runif(n + 2) > 0.4, round(rnorm(n + 2, 52, 10)), NA),   visit_year     = sample(c(2020, 2021), n + 2, replace = TRUE) )  # inject a few NAs in dsst_v1 too raw_data$dsst_v1[sample(seq_len(nrow(raw_data)), 15)] <- NA  dim(raw_data) #> [1] 102   9

title: Data Cleaning
text: Always look NA patterns delete anything. summarize_missingness() gives sorted view; drop_cols_by_missingness() removes columns threshold. fully_empty 100% missing almost_empty close . can drop threshold , say, 90%: drop_cols_by_missingness() prints removed; want view without modifying data, call summarize_missingness(raw_data, threshold = 90).
code: summarize_missingness(raw_data) #> # A tibble: 9 × 3 #>   column       count_na percent_na #>   <chr>           <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 fully_empty       102      100   #> 2 almost_empty      101       99.0 #> 3 dsst_v2            42       41.2 #> 4 dsst_v1            15       14.7 #> 5 subject_id          0        0   #> 6 age                 0        0   #> 7 bmi                 0        0   #> 8 weight_kg           0        0   #> 9 visit_year          0        0 data <- drop_cols_by_missingness(raw_data, threshold = 90) names(data) #> [1] "subject_id" "age"        "bmi"        "weight_kg"  "dsst_v1"    #> [6] "dsst_v2"    "visit_year"

title: Data Cleaning
text: synthetic data two duplicated subject IDs (S042, S073). remove_duplicates() defaults keeping row fewest NAs per duplicate ID — usually want one record complete . strategies keep = "first", "last", "none" (removes duplicates including originals — useful suspect duplicated ID data integrity problem).
code: data <- remove_duplicates(data, subject_id) nrow(data) #> [1] 100

title: Data Cleaning
text: dsst_v1 dsst_v2 measure collected two visits. Often want single coalesced column preferred source, plus tracking column showing visit contributed row. dsst_final takes first non-missing value across visits priority order specified; dsst_source records visit came . original dsst_v1 dsst_v2 preserved unchanged.
code: data <- combine_timepoints(   data,   base_names = "dsst",   timepoints = c("_v1", "_v2"),   timepoint_labels = c("_v1" = "Visit 1", "_v2" = "Visit 2") )  data %>%   select(subject_id, dsst_v1, dsst_v2, dsst_final, dsst_source) %>%   head() #> # A tibble: 6 × 5 #>   subject_id dsst_v1 dsst_v2 dsst_final dsst_source #>   <chr>        <dbl>   <dbl>      <dbl> <chr>       #> 1 S001            55      NA         55 Visit 1     #> 2 S002            47      NA         47 Visit 1     #> 3 S003            51      43         51 Visit 1     #> 4 S004            46      NA         46 Visit 1     #> 5 S005            46      43         46 Visit 1     #> 6 S006            38      57         38 Visit 1

title: Data Cleaning
text: weight_kg column 250 kg value ’s almost certainly data-entry error. replace_outliers_with_na() uses IQR rule (Tukey’s 1.5×IQR default) identify replace outliers NA. Two things worth knowing works: function idempotent — running twice produces result, since outliers become NA NA skipped subsequent passes. paired columns (e.g., weight log_weight), paired_cols argument nullifies paired entry whenever source column outlier, keeping derived columns consistent source. want inspect cutoffs without replacing anything, detect_outlier_thresholds() returns lower upper bounds:
code: summary(data$weight_kg) #>    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.  #>   39.30   67.15   74.40   74.58   84.08  108.00  data <- replace_outliers_with_na(   data,   var_names  = "weight_kg",   multiplier = 1.5 )  summary(data$weight_kg) #>    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.  #>   48.90   67.20   74.70   74.94   84.25  108.00 detect_outlier_thresholds(data, "weight_kg", multiplier = 1.5) #>       LB      UB     notes #> 1 41.625 109.825 weight_kg

title: Data Cleaning
text: Many downstream analyses want categorical age groups, BMI categories, etc. bin_and_categorize_variables() handles three transformation types — cutpoints (continuous), categorical mappings, custom functions — single interface. note bin semantics: cutpoints interpreted cut()’s left-open right-closed rule, except leftmost interval closed ends (matching include.lowest = TRUE). cutpoints c(18.5, 25, 30), BMI exactly 25 falls Normal, Overweight.
code: data <- bin_and_categorize_variables(data, groups = list(   list(     col       = "age",     type      = "cutpoints",     cutpoints = c(60, 75),     labels    = c("Younger", "Middle", "Older"),     name      = "age_group"   ),   list(     col       = "bmi",     type      = "cutpoints",     cutpoints = c(18.5, 25, 30),     labels    = c("Underweight", "Normal", "Overweight", "Obese"),     name      = "bmi_category"   ) ))  data %>%   count(age_group, bmi_category) #> # A tibble: 10 × 3 #>    age_group bmi_category     n #>    <fct>     <fct>        <int> #>  1 Younger   Normal           8 #>  2 Younger   Overweight      13 #>  3 Younger   Obese            1 #>  4 Middle    Underweight      2 #>  5 Middle    Normal          16 #>  6 Middle    Overweight      31 #>  7 Middle    Obese           16 #>  8 Older     Underweight      1 #>  9 Older     Normal           3 #> 10 Older     Overweight       8

title: Data Cleaning
text: whole pipeline reads cleanly single chain:
code: clean_data <- raw_data %>%   drop_cols_by_missingness(threshold = 90) %>%   remove_duplicates(subject_id) %>%   combine_timepoints(     base_names = "dsst",     timepoints = c("_v1", "_v2")   ) %>%   replace_outliers_with_na(var_names = "weight_kg") %>%   bin_and_categorize_variables(groups = list(     list(col = "age", type = "cutpoints", cutpoints = c(60, 75)),     list(col = "bmi", type = "cutpoints", cutpoints = c(18.5, 25, 30))   ))

title: Trajectory Classification from Mixed-Effects Models
text: Many longitudinal studies want ask: declining faster expected? natural quantity random slope mixed-effects model — subject’s deviation overall trend. continuous slope estimate isn’t directly usable group label, isn’t directly comparable across studies. Discretizing low / mid / high gives categorical predictor can drop downstream models, plot stratified group, report table. conceptual simplification think whether ’s appropriate analysis — slope noisy estimate, especially subjects visits, binning loses information. goal “find decliners describe look like,” ’s reasonable first move.

title: Trajectory Classification from Mixed-Effects Models
text: ’ll simulate 200 subjects 3–6 visits , shared linear trend, subject-specific random intercepts slopes.
code: set.seed(2024)  n_subjects <- 200  subjects <- tibble::tibble(   subject_id      = sprintf("sub_%03d", seq_len(n_subjects)),   baseline_age    = round(rnorm(n_subjects, 70, 6), 1),   random_intercept = rnorm(n_subjects, 0, 1),   random_slope    = rnorm(n_subjects, -0.4, 0.3)   # average decline of -0.4 / yr )  long_df <- subjects %>%   rowwise() %>%   mutate(     n_visits = sample(3:6, 1),     visits   = list(sort(round(c(0, runif(n_visits - 1, 1, 5)), 2)))   ) %>%   tidyr::unnest(visits) %>%   rename(time = visits) %>%   mutate(     score = 50 +       random_intercept +       (-0.4 + random_slope) * time +       rnorm(n(), 0, 1)   ) %>%   select(subject_id, baseline_age, time, score) %>%   ungroup()  head(long_df, 10) #> # A tibble: 10 × 4 #>    subject_id baseline_age  time score #>    <chr>             <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #>  1 sub_001            75.9  0     48.9 #>  2 sub_001            75.9  1.67  48.6 #>  3 sub_001            75.9  2.11  49.0 #>  4 sub_001            75.9  4.51  50.6 #>  5 sub_002            72.8  0     49.2 #>  6 sub_002            72.8  2.73  50.0 #>  7 sub_002            72.8  3.5   48.4 #>  8 sub_002            72.8  4.13  46.2 #>  9 sub_003            69.4  0     50.3 #> 10 sub_003            69.4  1.65  48.1

title: Trajectory Classification from Mixed-Effects Models
text: Standard random-intercepts--slopes specification: subject gets intercept slope time. fixed effect time recovers average decline (~-0.4). want classification per-subject deviation average, lives random effects.
code: fit <- lmer(score ~ time + (1 + time | subject_id), data = long_df) summary(fit)$coefficients #>              Estimate Std. Error   t value #> (Intercept) 50.133113 0.08417970 595.54874 #> time        -0.819343 0.03298606 -24.83907

title: Trajectory Classification from Mixed-Effects Models
text: classify_trajectory_groups() accepts subset "1sd", "1.5sd", "tertile", "quartile". Asking four lets us compare side side. output one row per subject, random intercept (model one), raw random slope, z-scored slope, one factor column per requested method.
code: trajectories <- classify_trajectory_groups(   fit,   id_col     = "subject_id",   slope_term = "time",   methods    = c("1sd", "1.5sd", "tertile", "quartile"),   labels     = list(low = "Decliner", mid = "Stable", high = "Improver"),   prefix     = "traj" )  head(trajectories) #> # A tibble: 6 × 8 #>   subject_id random_intercept random_slope slope_z traj_1sd traj_1.5sd #>   <chr>                 <dbl>        <dbl>   <dbl> <fct>    <fct>      #> 1 sub_001             -0.231         0.592   2.22  Improver Improver   #> 2 sub_002              0.0402        0.163   0.610 Stable   Stable     #> 3 sub_003             -0.190        -0.161  -0.603 Stable   Stable     #> 4 sub_004              0.0571       -0.348  -1.30  Decliner Stable     #> 5 sub_005              0.681         0.350   1.31  Improver Stable     #> 6 sub_006             -0.829        -0.427  -1.60  Decliner Decliner   #> # ℹ 2 more variables: traj_tertile <fct>, traj_quartile <fct>

title: Trajectory Classification from Mixed-Effects Models
text: four methods answer subtly different questions: SD-based (1sd, 1.5sd) thresholds z-scored slope. Subjects 1 (1.5) SDs group mean flagged. cutoffs anchored spread data — fewer subjects fall extreme groups distribution tight. Quantile-based (tertile, quartile) thresholds raw slope guarantees fixed share bucket: ~33% / 34% / 33% tertiles, ~25% / 50% / 25% quartiles. quick comparison group sizes: 1sd flags ~32% subjects extreme (16% tail); 1.5sd much stricter. Tertiles guarantee roughly equal-sized groups regardless slope distribution. pick? depends downstream question: want roughly balanced groups stratified plotting cross-tabulation: tertiles quartiles. want flag unusual trajectories “unusual” defined relative cohort’s spread: 1sd 1.5sd. ’re sure, run two methods check downstream conclusions don’t hinge choice. Stable conclusions trustworthy ones flip tertile 1.5sd.
code: trajectories %>%   count(traj_1sd) %>%   rename(group = traj_1sd, n_1sd = n) %>%   full_join(     trajectories %>% count(traj_1.5sd) %>% rename(group = traj_1.5sd, n_1.5sd = n),     by = "group"   ) %>%   full_join(     trajectories %>% count(traj_tertile) %>% rename(group = traj_tertile, n_tertile = n),     by = "group"   ) %>%   full_join(     trajectories %>% count(traj_quartile) %>% rename(group = traj_quartile, n_quartile = n),     by = "group"   ) #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   group    n_1sd n_1.5sd n_tertile n_quartile #>   <fct>    <int>   <int>     <int>      <int> #> 1 Stable     142     174        66        100 #> 2 Decliner    31      15        67         50 #> 3 Improver    27      11        67         50

title: Trajectory Classification from Mixed-Effects Models
text: whole point classifying usually feed groups something else. ’ll merge trajectory labels back subject-level table use fit_models_by_group() look baseline age varies trajectory. traj_tertile coefficients contrasts reference level (Stable default — classify_trajectory_groups() puts mid first analyses contrast extremes typical).
code: subject_level <- long_df %>%   group_by(subject_id, baseline_age) %>%   summarise(.groups = "drop") %>%   left_join(trajectories, by = "subject_id")  age_by_traj <- fit_models_by_group(   data            = subject_level,   outcomes        = "baseline_age",   base_predictors = "traj_tertile",   quiet           = TRUE )  extract_coefficients(age_by_traj) #> # A tibble: 3 × 15 #>   outcome      group model predictors   model_equation  term  estimate std.error #>   <chr>        <chr> <chr> <chr>        <chr>           <chr>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 baseline_age All   main  traj_tertile baseline_age ~… (Int…   69.1       0.753 #> 2 baseline_age All   main  traj_tertile baseline_age ~… traj…    2.17      1.06  #> 3 baseline_age All   main  traj_tertile baseline_age ~… traj…    0.979     1.06  #> # ℹ 7 more variables: statistic <dbl>, p.value <dbl>, model_error <chr>, #> #   n_obs <int>, conf.low <dbl>, conf.high <dbl>, sig <lgl>

title: Trajectory Classification from Mixed-Effects Models
text: lme4::isSingular(fit) TRUE, model summary reports near-zero variance slope term, random slopes unstable classifications based . classify_trajectory_groups() warn case still return result — warning cue revisit model, silently use output. see singularity, common fixes include simplifying random-effects structure, increasing number visits per subject (control data collection), accepting trajectory classification isn’t well-supported data.
code: isSingular(fit) #> [1] FALSE

title: REML Meta-analysis
text: ’ll simulate discovery cohort replication cohort, outcomes predictors, slightly different sample sizes effect magnitudes. mirrors common pattern genetic biomarker studies: estimate effects one population, test whether hold another.
code: set.seed(42)  simulate_cohort <- function(n, cohort_label, effect_age, effect_treatment) {   tibble::tibble(     subject_id = paste0(cohort_label, "_", seq_len(n)),     cohort     = cohort_label,     age        = round(rnorm(n, mean = 65, sd = 8), 1),     sex        = sample(c("F", "M"), n, replace = TRUE),     treatment  = factor(sample(c("control", "active"), n, replace = TRUE),                         levels = c("control", "active")),     nfl = effect_age * scale(age)[, 1] +                   effect_treatment * (treatment == "active") +                   rnorm(n, sd = 1),     ab42 = 0.5 * scale(age)[, 1] +                   0.2 * (treatment == "active") +                   rnorm(n, sd = 1)   ) }  discovery   <- simulate_cohort(n = 250, "discovery",   effect_age = 0.40, effect_treatment = 0.55)  replication <- simulate_cohort(n = 180, "replication", effect_age = 0.35, effect_treatment = 0.45)  combined <- bind_rows(discovery, replication)  head(combined) #> # A tibble: 6 × 7 #>   subject_id  cohort      age sex   treatment     nfl    ab42 #>   <chr>       <chr>     <dbl> <chr> <fct>       <dbl>   <dbl> #> 1 discovery_1 discovery  76   F     active    2.15    -0.693  #> 2 discovery_2 discovery  60.5 F     active    1.24    -2.03   #> 3 discovery_3 discovery  67.9 M     active    0.705    0.0556 #> 4 discovery_4 discovery  70.1 M     control   0.406    0.512  #> 5 discovery_5 discovery  68.2 F     active    0.00229 -1.86   #> 6 discovery_6 discovery  64.2 M     active    0.319    0.450

text: pooling, ’s worth confirming two cohorts share set group/term combinations plan analyze. check_cohort_alignment() flags combinations present one cohort. perfect match happy path. real analysis, step often catches issues like category coded "Control" one cohort "control" , level present one site.
code: check_cohort_alignment(   discovery, replication,   by      = c("treatment", "sex"),   x_label = "discovery",   y_label = "replication" )

what: Step 2: Fit models in each cohort
title: REML Meta-analysis
text: fit_models_by_group() fits linear model every (outcome, group) combination. passing group_col = "cohort", get one model per outcome per cohort — exactly need pooling. returned tibble list-columns fitted model object (model_obj) tidy results (model_res), plus data subset used (dataf). pooling, need tidy coefficients.
code: results <- fit_models_by_group(   data            = combined,   outcomes        = c("nfl", "ab42"),   base_predictors = c("age", "sex", "treatment"),   group_col       = "cohort",   groups          = c("discovery", "replication"),   quiet           = TRUE )  results %>%   select(outcome, cohort, model_equation, n_obs) #> # A tibble: 4 × 4 #>   outcome cohort      model_equation               n_obs #>   <chr>   <chr>       <chr>                        <int> #> 1 nfl     discovery   nfl ~ age + sex + treatment    250 #> 2 nfl     replication nfl ~ age + sex + treatment    180 #> 3 ab42    discovery   ab42 ~ age + sex + treatment   250 #> 4 ab42    replication ab42 ~ age + sex + treatment   180

title: REML Meta-analysis
text: extract_coefficients() unnests model_res adds confidence intervals computed standard errors. can filter specific term — , treatment effect. now one row per cohort per outcome — input shape add_meta_pooled_results() expects.
code: treatment_effects <- extract_coefficients(   results,   term_pattern = "treatmentactive" )  treatment_effects %>%   select(outcome, cohort, term, estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) #> # A tibble: 4 × 8 #>   outcome cohort      term         estimate std.error conf.low conf.high p.value #>   <chr>   <chr>       <chr>           <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl> #> 1 nfl     discovery   treatmentac…   0.590      0.126    0.342     0.837 5.03e-6 #> 2 nfl     replication treatmentac…   0.388      0.141    0.112     0.664 6.44e-3 #> 3 ab42    discovery   treatmentac…   0.0588     0.137   -0.210     0.327 6.68e-1 #> 4 ab42    replication treatmentac…   0.172      0.155   -0.132     0.476 2.70e-1

title: REML Meta-analysis
text: add_meta_pooled_results() runs random-effects meta-analysis (REML via metafor::rma()) within group defined group_vars, appends pooled row, adds weight column showing cohort’s percent contribution pooled estimate. things notice output: weight filled cohort rows (REML contribution percent) NA pooled row. I2, Q, tau2 heterogeneity statistics, attached pooled row. same_direction TRUE discovery replication estimates sign. replicated TRUE () signs agree (b) replication estimate |β/SE| > 1.96.
code: pooled <- add_meta_pooled_results(   treatment_effects,   group_vars = c("outcome", "term") )  pooled %>%   select(outcome, cohort, estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high,          weight, I2, same_direction, replicated) #> # A tibble: 6 × 10 #>   outcome cohort      estimate std.error conf.low conf.high weight    I2 #>   <chr>   <fct>          <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 ab42    discovery     0.0588     0.137  -0.210      0.327   56.2  NA   #> 2 ab42    replication   0.172      0.155  -0.132      0.476   43.8  NA   #> 3 ab42    Pooled        0.108      0.103  -0.0930     0.310   NA     0   #> 4 nfl     discovery     0.590      0.126   0.342      0.837   54.8  NA   #> 5 nfl     replication   0.388      0.141   0.112      0.664   45.2  NA   #> 6 nfl     Pooled        0.499      0.100   0.302      0.695   NA    11.7 #> # ℹ 2 more variables: same_direction <lgl>, replicated <lgl>

title: REML Meta-analysis
text: replicated flag useful heuristic formal test. captures common-sense idea finding replicates points direction cohorts reaches roughly nominal significance second. Two caveats: applies k = 2. k ≥ 3 cohorts, sign agreement “replication estimate” stop well-defined concepts, columns set NA. three cohorts, lean pooled p-value, I2, cohort-level CIs instead. 1.96 threshold treats replication cohort separate significance test rather building joint replication statistic. Methods like Sidak-corrected replication test stricter; flag best read “directionally consistent individually significant replication.”

title: REML Meta-analysis

title: Upgrading from 1.x to 2.0
text: Three goals drove 2.0 cleanup: Consistent naming across package. Verbs like quiet (suppress messages) nouns like classify_trajectory_groups (function ) replace older mixed conventions (verbose, categorize_slopes). single validation interface. old validate_params worked couldn’t extended cleanly. validate_args exported is_*() assertion helpers replace composable pieces. Tighter scope. Caching utilities outlier detection moved packages actually belong; meta-analysis weights now computed automatically rather separate step.

text: listed NEWS.md worth flagging change behavior, just APIs: compare_coefs() now uses 2 * pnorm(-abs(z)) instead 2 * (1 - pnorm(abs(z))). P-values highly significant differences (|z| > ~8) previously underflowed 0 now computed accurately. bin_and_categorize_variables() applies categorical mappings single pass. mapping like c("" = "B", "B" = "C"), old version may chained remap (→ B → C); new version (→ B, B → C). convert_columns_to_factors() preserves unobserved factor levels promoting existing factor ordered. match pattern code, double-check output upgrading.

title: Log-Transformation and Z-Scores
text: Many analyses need numeric measures put common, comparable scale modeling. usual sequence handle outliers → transform skewed variables → standardize, adRutils provides function step, add new columns rather overwriting originals every stage easy inspect reverse. pattern applies set skewed, differently-scaled numeric measures — assay panels, lab chemistries, gene-expression values, questionnaire subscales, . use small set plasma biomarkers running example exercise every step (right-skewed distributions different numeric scales), nothing workflow specific biomarkers. result set standardized variables (mean 0, SD 1) suitable comparison combining; outlier log steps preparation makes final standardization robust. Data cleaning pipeline vignette covers upstream steps — missingness, duplicates, binning. one picks transform--standardize stage.
code: library(adRutils) library(dplyr)

title: Log-Transformation and Z-Scores
text: Three measures different scales: marker_a hundreds, marker_b marker_c small right-skewed.
code: set.seed(7) n <- 60  measures <- tibble::tibble(   subject_id = sprintf("S%03d", 1:n),   ancestry   = sample(c("EUR", "AFR"), n, replace = TRUE),   marker_a   = round(rnorm(n, 900, 150), 1),   marker_b   = round(rlnorm(n, log(2),  0.5), 2),   marker_c   = round(rlnorm(n, log(15), 0.6), 1) )  # inject one implausible value to demonstrate outlier handling measures$marker_c[1] <- 500  head(measures) #> # A tibble: 6 × 5 #>   subject_id ancestry marker_a marker_b marker_c #>   <chr>      <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 S001       AFR          769.     4.53    500   #> 2 S002       EUR         1008.     1.45     29   #> 3 S003       EUR          917.     2.73      8.7 #> 4 S004       AFR          888.     2.25     13.2 #> 5 S005       EUR          837.     3.05     22.5 #> 6 S006       AFR          816.     1.5       9.3

title: Log-Transformation and Z-Scores
text: replace_outliers_with_na() flags values outside Tukey IQR fence sets NA. Apply three measures . inspect cutoffs without altering data, detect_outlier_thresholds() returns bounds single column:
code: measures <- replace_outliers_with_na(   measures,   var_names          = c("marker_a", "marker_b", "marker_c"),   multiplier         = 1.5,   remove_all_na_rows = FALSE ) #> ✔ Replaced 5 outliers with NA across 3 variables detect_outlier_thresholds(measures, "marker_c", multiplier = 1.5) #>        LB      UB    notes #> 1 -7.9375 41.1625 marker_c

title: Log-Transformation and Z-Scores
text: transform_log10() adds log10_<var> columns leaves originals place. requires strictly positive input, suits concentration-style measures.
code: measures <- transform_log10(   measures,   vars = c("marker_a", "marker_b", "marker_c") )  names(measures) #> [1] "subject_id"     "ancestry"       "marker_a"       "marker_b"       #> [5] "marker_c"       "log10_marker_a" "log10_marker_b" "log10_marker_c"

title: Log-Transformation and Z-Scores
text: Standardizing log-scaled measures z-scores (mean 0, SD 1) puts common scale can compared combined. standardize log10_ columns Step 2. distributions differ systematically across grouping variable, standardizing within strata keeps group internally comparable. Pass group_vars:
code: log_vars <- c("log10_marker_a", "log10_marker_b", "log10_marker_c")  measures <- compute_zscores(   measures,   vars   = log_vars,   prefix = "z_" ) #> ℹ Standardizing 3 variables #> ✔ Standardization complete (3 variables processed)  measures %>%   select(subject_id, dplyr::starts_with("z_")) %>%   head() #> # A tibble: 6 × 4 #>   subject_id z_log10_marker_a z_log10_marker_b z_log10_marker_c #>   <chr>                 <dbl>            <dbl>            <dbl> #> 1 S001               -1.04              1.67             NA     #> 2 S002                0.920            -0.931             1.14  #> 3 S003                0.231             0.513            -0.970 #> 4 S004                0.00181           0.0718           -0.238 #> 5 S005               -0.431             0.766             0.698 #> 6 S006               -0.617            -0.853            -0.853 measures <- compute_zscores(   measures,   vars       = log_vars,   prefix     = "z_within_ancestry_",   group_vars = "ancestry" ) #> ℹ Standardizing 3 variables by 1 grouping variable #> ✔ Standardization complete (3 variables processed)

code: standardized <- raw_measures %>%   replace_outliers_with_na(     var_names          = c("marker_a", "marker_b", "marker_c"),     remove_all_na_rows = FALSE   ) %>%   transform_log10(vars = c("marker_a", "marker_b", "marker_c")) %>%   compute_zscores(     vars   = c("log10_marker_a", "log10_marker_b", "log10_marker_c"),     prefix = "z_"   )

text: data tidy effects data frame containing minimum cohort, estimate, std.error, n_obs columns. group_vars Character vector grouping columns (e.g., c("outcome", "term")). cohort_col Name cohort identifier column. Default "cohort". discovery_label, replication_label Labels identifying discovery replication cohorts, used computing same_direction replicated k = 2 groups. NULL (default), first second cohort values (appearance order) used. pooled_label Label used appended pooled row. Default "Pooled". cohort_levels Optional character vector defining factor levels cohort column returned data. NULL, factor built unique values pooled_label last.

title: Run REML meta-analysis within groups and append pooled rows — add_meta_pooled_results
text: data pooled rows appended weight column added. New columns pooled rows: I2, Q, tau2, n_cohorts, same_direction, replicated. pooled row's n_obs sum across cohorts (total subjects, effective sample size).

title: Run REML meta-analysis within groups and append pooled rows — add_meta_pooled_results
text: Heterogeneity statistics (I2, Q, tau2) attached pooled row k >= 2. exactly two cohorts present group, same_direction (sign agreement) replicated (sign agreement |β/SE| > 1.96 second cohort) computed pooled row. concepts generalize k >= 3, set NA case. Pooling requires least 2 cohorts data overall; input one cohort treated misuse aborts immediately. Within multi-cohort dataset, individual (group_vars) cells may still one cohort represented (e.g., model failed fit cohort, input unbalanced). cells retained -, pooled row appended weight = NA, warning identifies cells skipped. Confidence intervals pooled row computed REML model stored conf.low/conf.high (broom convention). input data uses CI column names (e.g., lci/uci), columns treated metadata carried forward unchanged first cohort row group, recomputed. Rename conf.low/conf.high calling get correct pooled CIs.

title: Bin and categorize variables into groups — bin_and_categorize_variables
text: dataf data frame. groups list group specifications. element list fields: col (required): Name column dataf transform. type (required): One "cutpoints", "categorical", "custom". name (optional): Output column name. Defaults <col>_group. cutpoints (required type = "cutpoints"): Numeric vector internal break points, increasing order. Intervals left-open, right-closed, except leftmost closed ends (matching base::cut() include.lowest = TRUE). labels (optional, type = "cutpoints"): Character vector length length(cutpoints) + 1. Defaults interval notation derived cutpoints. values (required type = "categorical"): Named vector mapping original values new labels. mappings applied simultaneously, chained remappings occur. custom_fn (required type = "custom"): function taking column vector returning vector length. filter_missing TRUE, drop rows NA newly created group column. Default FALSE. quiet TRUE, suppress informational messages. Default FALSE.

Default "time". methods Character vector. subset "1sd", "1.5sd", "tertile", "quartile". labels Named list elements low, mid, high. prefix Prefix output group columns. title: Classify subjects into trajectory groups from a mixed-effects model — classify_trajectory_groups

title: Classify subjects into trajectory groups from a mixed-effects model — classify_trajectory_groups
text: SD-based methods ("1sd", "1.5sd") threshold z-scored slope: subjects beyond ±1 (±1.5) SD group mean assigned low/high groups, rest mid. Quantile-based methods ("tertile", "quartile") threshold raw slope: lowest tertile (quartile) goes low, highest high, rest mid. Subjects exactly cutpoint assigned middle group. mid label default reference level since represents typical trajectory; downstream contrasts compare extreme groups typical one.

title: Validate function inputs with consistent error messages — validate_args
text: ... Named assertions, e.g. quiet = is_flag(). name must refer argument calling function. data Optional data frame. supplied, enables columns = numeric_columns = shorthand column existence/type checks. columns Character vector columns required exist data. numeric_columns Character vector columns required exist data numeric. custom_checks list custom_check() results (equivalent list(condition, message) pairs quoted conditions) domain-specific checks fit standard shapes. context Calling function name. NULL, inferred call stack. .envir environment look argument values. Defaults calling environment, correct typical use calling validate_args() directly inside function. Pass explicit environment wrapping validate_args() helper.

title: Validate function inputs with consistent error messages — validate_args
text: Invisibly TRUE checks pass; aborts consolidated error message otherwise.

title: Validate function inputs with consistent error messages — validate_args
text: Custom checks accept unevaluated expressions, safe reference values may NULL otherwise problematic — expression evaluated inside validate_args(), errors caught reported validation failures rather propagating caller. Use custom_check() (list(condition = ..., message = ...) pair, condition wrapped rlang::quo() call deferred):
code: validate_args(   x = is_string(),   custom_checks = list(     custom_check(nrow(data) > 0, "{.arg data} must have at least one row")   ) )
